title: Spotify co-CEOs talk ad challenges and plans for GenAI features - Music Ally
text:
Following
yesterday’s quarterly financial results
, Spotify’s new co-CEOs Alex Norström and Gustav Söderström
fielded questions from analysts in the company’s earnings call
. Challenges in its advertising business, some stats on key features, and plans for GenAI were on the agenda.
Norström addressed Spotify’s issues with advertising, admitting that the company has had to spend “a year and a half of rebuilding the foundation” of its ads business after being slow to adapt to shifts in how brands want to buy.
“Brands have always valued Spotify for its high user engagement, its beloved brand and its high quality content. But the market shifted with advertisers now favouring biddable buying [
primer here
]. We had to evolve to capture that… so we rebuilt our stack end to end,” he said.
“Now, while this creates some short term pressure, it unlocks a much larger opportunity. We are making solid progress. Today biddable represents more than a third of ad revenue and it’s growing quickly… and this rebuilt new stack that we have, it actually gives us more opportunity to do new things that we couldn’t do before.”
Söderström offered up some stats for some of Spotify’s key features later in the call. Its AI-powered DJ is “now used by 94 million subscribers, closing in on 100 million, driving billions of hours of engagement”.
Meanwhile the ‘Jam’ co-listening feature is also growing. “Usage has doubled year over year and now exceeds 100 million monthly listening hours,” said Söderström. Meanwhile, the
newly-launched ‘Song DNA’ feature
is “up to 52 million users in just four weeks”.
Another stat: Spotify now has a catalogue of around 250 million tracks – a sharp increase from the not-that-long-ago days when 100 million was the standard figure for DSPs on this metric.
Söderström revealed it after being asked about the explosion in AI-generated music being uploaded to streaming services. Which he thinks is… good. But for a specific reason.
“It’s new original music, which is happening at scale and quickly expanding the catalogue. And that we think is good for a company that aggregates content because it makes the recommendation problem even more important,” he said.
“I joined Spotify back in 2008. When I joined, I think the music catalogue was about two million tracks, and now it’s something like 250 million tracks. So the growth of the catalogue is not new. We think it’s going to keep increasing, and that means that the recommendation problem gets more important for consumers.”
Söderström also made it clear that Spotify’s own efforts around generative AI will focus on working with professional musicians.
“Right now existing creators are largely left out of the AI opportunity altogether. Many creators are using AI to make new music, but existing creators cannot join,” he said.
“That’s because the copyright problem is much more complicated to solve. Well, and the attribution problem of who should get paid what is is much harder. But we love hard problems, so that’s the problem we want to go after. We want to take this opportunity to existing creators as well, with derivatives of existing IP.”
He also made the case for Spotify not being under threat from disrupter competitors using AI – including (in the words of analyst William Packer, who asked the question) “AI-first alternatives which integrate label content”.
“We have about almost 20 years of listening history,” said Söderström. “Some of the things in the world are facts that can be easily commoditised by LLMs, such as the capital of Texas or something. Other things are not so easily commoditised.”
“And it turns out, luckily for us, taste is not easily commoditised because it’s not a fact, it’s an opinion. It differs between people, it differs between regions, it differs between people in those regions and use cases. And on top of that it changes weekly [based on] what is cool right now and what is culture.”
“If you theoretically said that someone even could somehow snapshot all our user data, all 700 million-plus users, they could train a model on that and then that model is pretty useless after about maybe two, three weeks as culture moved on.”
Stuart Dredge
Music Ally's Head of Insight
More by Stuart Dredge
